He traded in promises, each gift wrapped in fog, and exploited others with a smile that never touched honesty. His one clever betrayal, which worked only once, began with a forged seal. He claimed a royal decree would confiscate anyone who doubted him and grant safety to all who obeyed. A wary merchant paid with his precious ring, believing obedience would bring prosperity. The town thrived for a season; Nalo vanished amid applause, counting coins.

Detection came when a child heard the seal speak in a dream and recognized the handwriting as a sham. The seal’s voice and river spirit spoke together: the decree never existed. The river’s memory confronted Nalo in a storm night, showing him the chaos his deceit had seeded. The punishment was costly: the river demanded Nalo bear the weight of every lie as a living cloak, each thread a whispered name of someone betrayed, growing heavier and darker with time. He trudged through rain and shadow, unable to shed the cloak.

Yet the cycle ends as people choose truth over temptation, and Nalo’s cloak rusts away for good.
